context: They told to Mordecai Esther's words.
point_summary: Mordecai's rebuke to Esther illustrates that divine deliverance is certain regardless of human cooperation, since God will accomplish His purposes through another means if necessary.

context: Then Mordecai commanded to answer Esther, think not with thyself that thou shalt escape in the king's house more than all the Jews.
point_summary: Mordecai's rebuke to Esther — that her palace position offered no exemption from danger and that God would raise up deliverance through another if she refused — frames the call to embrace one's God-appointed place and moment with purposeful confidence.

context: for if thou altogether holdest thy peace at this time, then shall their enlargement and deliverance arise to the Jews from another place... and who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this?
point_summary: Mordecai's confidence that God would raise up deliverance from another place if Esther refused illustrates the sovereign providence underlying the call to embrace one's appointed moment and place.

context: look over the book of Esther, if you will. I want you to look at the eighth chapter
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: what honor and dignity has been done to Mordecai for this
point_summary: The king's discovery that Mordecai's loyalty went unrewarded sets in motion a divine reversal that ultimately exposes and destroys Haman, illustrating God's providential timing over those who threaten His people.

context: what shall be done in verse number six unto the man whom the king delighteth to honor
point_summary: Haman's self-serving assumption that the king's honor was meant for him illustrates how pride blinds a man to his own impending judgment, with God's providence working unseen against him.

context: Haman then is exposed in the next chapter, chapter number seven
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: on that day did the King Ahasuerus give the house of Haman, the Jews enemy unto Esther the queen
point_summary: Esther's intercession before the king—weeping and pleading for her people facing a death decree—illustrates the burden believers should carry for those who will face coming tribulation judgment.

context: Mordecai came before the king for Esther had told what he was unto her. The king took off his ring which he had taken from Haman and gave it unto Mordecai and Esther set Mordecai over the house of Haman
point_summary: Esther's tearful intercession before the king—driven by her inability to endure seeing her people destroyed—is held up as a model of the compassionate urgency believers should feel toward those who will face coming tribulation judgment.

context: For how can I endure to see the evil that shall come unto my people? Or how can I endure to see the destruction of my kindred?
point_summary: Esther's tearful intercession — "How can I endure to see the evil that shall come unto my people?" — is held up as a model of grief-driven urgency for the unsaved, paralleling the stakes of coming prophetic judgment.

context: She besought the king with tears. She said, how can I endure to see this?
point_summary: Esther's tearful intercession before the king illustrates the soul-burdened urgency believers should feel toward the lost who face coming tribulation judgment.

context: take your Bibles and turn to Esther chapter number eight. Esther chapter number eight
point_summary: Esther's tearful intercession before Ahasuerus — unable to endure seeing destruction come upon her people — is held up as a model of the urgency and compassion believers should feel toward the lost as tribulation judgment approaches.

context: last week we looked at a few verses in Esther as well
point_summary: Esther's tearful intercession before Ahasuerus — "how can I endure to see the destruction of my kindred?" — is cited as a model of the urgent compassion believers should feel toward the lost as tribulation judgment intensifies.

context: Esther, as she's standing before Ahasuerus in chapter number eight
point_summary: Esther's tearful intercession before Ahasuerus — "How can I endure to see the destruction of my kindred?" — is cited to stir urgency and compassion for the lost in light of coming tribulation judgment.

context: the Bible says that she besought him with tears in verse number three
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it says in verse number six, how can I endure to see the evil that shall come upon my people
point_summary: Esther's cry—"how can I endure to see the destruction of my kindred?"—is used to call believers to compassionate urgency for the lost before the tribulation's judgments fall.

reference_text: chapter eight of Esther in verse number nine
context: Let me look here in chapter eight of Esther in verse number nine, the Bible says, then were the king's scribes called at that time
point_summary: Esther 8:9's sweeping geographic reach—from India to Ethiopia, 127 provinces, every language—illustrates the urgency of getting a life-saving message out to every people without delay.

context: in chapter number three in verse number 12 is where Haman gets permission from Ahasuerus to write the first decree
point_summary: Esther 3:12 establishes the timeline of Haman's decree—issued the 13th day of the first month, with destruction set for the 13th of the twelfth—creating the urgent window within which the counter-decree of Esther 8 had to operate.

context: that same writing in Esther chapter number three that was in chapter number three, verse 12
point_summary: Esther 3:12 marks the date of Haman's decree—the 13th day of the first month—establishing the timeline against which the urgency of the counter-decree in chapter 8 is measured.

context: according to verse number 13 of Esther chapter number three
point_summary: Esther 3:13 establishes the date of the Jews' decreed destruction—the 13th day of the 12th month—creating the urgent eight-and-a-half-month window within which the counter-decree of Esther 8 had to reach all 127 provinces.

context: So you come to Esther chapter number eight and you find that the Bible tells us that this writing often being referred to as the counter decree was written on the 23rd day of the third month
point_summary: The counter-decree of Esther 8, written on the 23rd day of the third month, left only roughly eight and a half months to reach 127 provinces spanning India to Ethiopia, demanding urgent action before the first decree's destruction date.

context: The copy of the writing for a commandment to be given, notice what it says, in every province was published unto all people and that the Jews should be ready against that day to avenge themselves on their enemies
point_summary: The counter-decree of Esther 8:13 illustrates the urgency required to warn all people across a vast empire before a fixed deadline of destruction arrived.

context: so the post that rode upon mules and camels went out
point_summary: The urgency embedded in Esther 8:14 — riders on mules and camels going out "hastened and pressed" — illustrates that the vast geographic scope of the Persian empire demanded immediate, relentless action to outrun a fixed deadline.

context: they went out being hastened and pressed
point_summary: The urgency embedded in Esther 8:14 — riders on mules and camels going out "hastened and pressed" — illustrates that the vast geographic scope of the Persian empire demanded immediate, relentless action to outrun a fixed deadline.

context: why do I come over here to Esther chapter number eight as we begin to think about what we're studying in the book of Revelation
point_summary: In contrast to the Esther narrative where a known calendar date drove urgent action, believers today face the doctrine of imminence—no fixed date—making gospel urgency all the more pressing.

context: the writing of Ahasuerus that Haman signed
point_summary: The urgency of Esther's counter-decree—dispatched with haste against a fixed date of destruction—illustrates that knowledge of coming judgment must compel immediate action, not procrastination.

context: there's a people in your kingdom and they're going to be put to death
point_summary: The decree of annihilation in Esther, with its fixed calendar date, illustrates the urgency that knowledge of coming judgment demands — leaving no room for procrastination in gospel witness.

context: 127 provinces
point_summary: The 127 provinces under Haman's death decree illustrate the urgency that knowledge of a fixed, coming judgment demands — leaving no room for procrastination in response.

context: I'm reminded this evening, as I think about this word urgency, with a wonderful example from the book of Esther
point_summary: Haman's decree against the Jews, with its fixed calendar date, illustrates the urgency that should mark believers who know prophetic judgment is coming and cannot afford to procrastinate.

context: in Esther chapter 8 verses 13 and 14
point_summary: The urgency of delivering the counter-decree in Esther 8 to all 127 provinces before a fixed day of destruction illustrates the ambassador's urgency to publish the message of reconciliation before people enter eternity.

context: if you read in Esther chapter 8 verses in 13 and 14 you see how that they went out with haste
point_summary: The urgency with which Mordecai's counter-decree was published and dispatched to 127 provinces illustrates the haste with which gospel ambassadors must carry the message of salvation to a lost world.

context: if you read in Esther chapter 8 verses in 13 and 14 you see how that they went out with haste
point_summary: The urgent, haste-driven delivery of the counter-decree to 127 provinces illustrates the urgency ambassadors of the gospel must have in publishing the message of reconciliation to a lost world.
